name: Pentominoes
author: Solomon W. Golomb
description: Solomon W. Golomb's pentomino game (mid-1950s), popularised by Martin Gardner. Players alternate placing pentominoes on an empty 8x8 board, covering whole squares and without overlap. The twelve pieces are a SINGLE SHARED POOL — each is used at most once, by either player — so both players always have the same moves and the game is impartial (like Cram). Pieces are the twelve FREE pentominoes: rotations and reflections of a piece are the same piece. Normal play: the player who cannot make a move loses; there are no draws, and a game lasts at most 12 placements. There are exactly 2308 opening moves (296 discounting the board's 8 symmetries). Orman proved in 1996, by examining some 22 billion positions, that Pentominoes is a first-player win.
